notes.anchor: If that man is going to enter Rome with restraint
notes.note: The unnamed 'that man' is Caesar, whose advance down Italy after crossing the Rubicon in January 49 BC is the crisis of the whole letter; Cicero is at Minturnae weighing whether his wife and daughter are safe in Rome.
T. Labienus, Caesar's ablest legate in Gaul, defected to Pompey and the Senate at the war's outbreak; Cicero treats the desertion as a hopeful sign for the Republican side.

notes.anchor: Piso too is a help, in that he is leaving the city and condemns his own son-in-law of crime
notes.note: L. Calpurnius Piso Caesoninus, consul of 58 BC, was Caesar's father-in-law (Caesar had married his daughter Calpurnia); his public disapproval and departure from Rome lent moral weight to the senatorial cause.

A town in southern Latium on the Via Appia near the mouth of the Liris, on Cicero's route between Rome and his Campanian estates; he wrote from here while shadowing the early movements of the civil war.
